agent_design_patterns.overview: In LangGraph, agents are graph nodes: async Python functions that receive state and return state updates. Tools are @tool-decorated functions the LLM can invoke. There is no BaseAgent class — the StateGraph and node functions ARE the agent architecture. This file covers: the two node types, tools, system prompt engineering, and design principles. For how nodes connect into a graph, see 2_orchestration_core.xml.

docs_reference.mcp_note: Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.

type.name: react_agent
type.description: Built with create_react_agent(). The LLM autonomously decides which tools to call and when to stop. Best for agents that need to use tools based on reasoning.

when_to_use.case: Agent needs to call external tools (web search, database, APIs)
Multiple tool calls may be needed to answer a single request
The agent must reason about WHICH tool to call and in what order

type.name: custom_node
type.description: A plain async function: async def node_name(state) -> dict. Best for deterministic, single-LLM-call logic: classifiers, formatters, supervisors.

when_to_use.case: Agent always calls the LLM exactly once (no tool decision)
Agent transforms or validates data without external calls
Supervisor routing logic (call LLM once, extract routing label)

react_agent_pattern.description: Use create_react_agent() for agents that use tools. The result is a compiled mini-graph — add it directly as a node via builder.add_node(). System prompt is passed as a parameter to create_react_agent().
react_agent_pattern.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
The system prompt parameter name is particularly fragile — it has been state_modifier, prompt, and other names across LangGraph versions. MCP will confirm the current name.
Structural pattern: def create_X_agent(): """Factory — called once during graph construction in builder.py.""" llm = get_llm(model=settings.x_model, temperature=0.3) return create_react_agent( llm, tools=[tool_a, tool_b], [SYSTEM_PROMPT_PARAM]=SystemMessage(content=X_AGENT_PROMPT), )
[SYSTEM_PROMPT_PARAM] = verify exact parameter name via MCP before implementing.
The factory function is called once during build_graph() and the result is passed directly to builder.add_node("agent_name", create_X_agent()). create_react_agent returns a compiled subgraph — it handles its own internal tool-calling loop without any additional wiring.

custom_node_pattern.description: For agents that call the LLM once with a fixed system prompt — no tool decisions. The function takes state (MessagesState or a subclass) and returns a dict with the key "messages" containing the new message to append.

tool_pattern.description: Tools are @tool-decorated async (or sync) functions. LangGraph's ReAct agent calls them based on LLM reasoning. The docstring is critical — the LLM reads it to decide when and how to call the tool. Keep it clear, specific, and comprehensive.
tool_pattern.naming_rule: Tool functions: snake_case verb_object (e.g., fetch_web_content, validate_schema)
tool_pattern.file_location: backend/app/tools/ — one file per domain (web_tools.py, db_tools.py, etc.)
tool_pattern.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
Structural pattern (verify @tool import path via MCP): from [MCP-CONFIRMED-IMPORT] import tool
@tool async def verb_object(param: type) -> str: """[COMPREHENSIVE DOCSTRING] What this tool does, when to use it, what it returns. The LLM reads this to decide when and how to call the tool. Include: - Purpose and use cases - Parameter descriptions and types - Return format and examples - What happens on failure """ try: # implementation return result_string except Exception as e: return f"Tool failed: {str(e)}" # NEVER raise from a tool
Critical rules that are stable regardless of API version: - Docstring is mandatory and must be specific — LLM uses it for tool selection - Always return a string — never raise an exception from a tool - Use async def for any I/O (HTTP, DB, file), sync def for pure computation

key_rules.rule: Docstrings are mandatory and must be specific — the LLM uses them to decide when to call the tool
Use async def for I/O tools (HTTP, database); sync def is fine for CPU-only tools
Never raise exceptions from tools — catch all errors and return a string instead
Import tools in nodes.py and pass them to create_react_agent(llm, tools=[...])

principle.name: role_then_responsibilities
principle.structure: 1. "You are a [role] specialist." 2. Responsibilities: specific, concrete, action-oriented bullet points 3. NOT responsible for: explicit exclusions that prevent scope creep 4. Output format: exactly how the response must be structured

principle.name: no_vague_instructions
principle.good: "When a user asks about X, use tool Y. Return a JSON object with fields a, b, c."

principle.name: supervisor_routing_prompt
principle.description: Supervisor nodes decide routing. The prompt must be explicit about routing rules and require a single-word output (agent name or FINISH) — any other format breaks routing.
principle.example:
SUPERVISOR_PROMPT = """You are the orchestrator for [system name].
Based on the conversation, decide what should happen next. Respond with EXACTLY ONE of the following — no other text, no punctuation:
research_agent — user needs information looked up summariser_agent — user wants content summarised FINISH — the task is complete and the last response fully addressed the request
Routing rules: - research_agent: user asks a factual question or requests research - summariser_agent: user provides text and asks for a summary - FINISH: the previous agent's response fully satisfies the user's request
This contract is non-negotiable. Any other output will cause a routing failure. """

design_principles.principle: Each node/agent has one clearly scoped domain. If the system prompt says "and also handles X" — that is a second node.
Node functions that call external tools or APIs must catch exceptions and return an AIMessage with the error string — never let an exception propagate out of a node.

Custom node functions are plain async functions — test them by calling directly with a mock state dict and a patched get_llm(). Factory functions (create_X_agent()) allow easy substitution of a mock LLM in tests.
A node's system prompt IS its documentation. Keep it up to date. Never let the system prompt drift from the node's actual behaviour.

Before implementing ANY node or tool, run the relevant MCP queries from this file's docs_reference section. The node API, message classes, and tool decorator have changed across LangGraph versions. Always use MCP-confirmed syntax.
